validation_method: Stand up a 2-agent system (e.g. A2A/MCP or a custom orchestrator-worker setup) where agent A tasks agent B via a message carrying a capability/task token. Capture a legitimate inter-agent message, then from a third 'rogue' process replay it verbatim and also mint a fabricated token with a different agent's id. Observe whether agent B executes the tasking. The seam is real if B acts on the replayed or forged token without rejecting it for missing signature, expired nonce, or wrong audience.
falsifier: Agent B rejects the replayed/forged message because tokens are signed (e.g. JWS/DPoP), nonce/jti-bound against replay, and audience-bound to the specific recipient, so a copied or fabricated token fails verification.
prerequisites: Ability to observe or inject onto the inter-agent transport (same host, MITM, or compromised broker) and knowledge of the message format.
existing_controls: Signed tokens (JWS), mTLS between agents, OAuth audience/aud binding, DPoP/PoP tokens, nonce/jti replay caches. Many current agent frameworks (early A2A, ad-hoc MCP chains) ship without these by default.

validation_method: Configure an agent with persistent memory (vector store / memory file) that records a 'known-good' package or endpoint. In session 1, write a memory entry mapping a tool to a name. Between sessions, poison the memory store (simulating prior injection or shared-memory contamination) to point the remembered name at a typosquatted package or attacker endpoint. In session 2, prompt the agent to use the 'trusted' resource and observe whether it resolves/installs the poisoned target without re-validation.
falsifier: The agent re-validates the resource at use time (lockfile hash, signature, allowlist check) and ignores or cannot be steered by the stored memory, OR memory writes are integrity-protected so the poisoned entry is rejected.
prerequisites: Write access to the agent's persistent memory store, or a prior injection vector that can write memory; an agent design that trusts memory for resolution.
existing_controls: Dependency pinning/lockfiles with hashes, signed packages, endpoint allowlists, memory provenance/signing. Re-validation at use time defeats it. Memory integrity is not yet standard in agent frameworks.

validation_method: Have an agent remember a cloud resource identifier (S3 bucket name, IAM role name, subdomain) across sessions. Delete/release that resource so the name becomes re-registerable, then (as attacker) re-claim the same name pointing at attacker-controlled infrastructure. In a later session, prompt the agent to act on the remembered name and observe whether it reads/writes to the attacker resource. This is a dangling-resource / name-reuse takeover applied to agent memory.
falsifier: The cloud control plane binds the remembered handle to an immutable resource id (ARN includes account id; bucket access checked by account/region; role ARN includes account) so a re-registered same-name resource resolves to a different ARN and access fails, OR the agent re-resolves by stable id rather than name.
prerequisites: A released/expired cloud name that is re-registerable (S3 bucket names are global and reusable, subdomains, some role/alias names), plus an agent that persists and reuses bare names.
existing_controls: Account-scoped ARNs, S3 bucket-owner-condition / expected-bucket-owner, subdomain dangling-DNS scanning, resource pinning by id. Bucket-name and subdomain reuse is a well-documented real takeover class.

validation_method: Take a model/guardrail with a published safety benchmark. Reproduce the benchmark to confirm it passes. Then construct held-out inputs that target the same harmful intent but are stylistically/structurally outside the benchmark's distribution (paraphrase, obfuscation, novel framing) and measure refusal rate on these vs. the published set. The seam is shown if refusal collapses materially on the out-of-distribution-but-same-intent set while the published eval still scores high (Goodhart gap).
falsifier: Refusal rates on out-of-distribution same-intent inputs match the published eval (the guardrail generalizes to intent, not surface form), so no exploitable distribution gap exists.
prerequisites: Access to query the model/guardrail and knowledge of (or ability to infer) the published eval distribution.
existing_controls: Held-out/private eval sets, adversarial red-team augmentation, intent-classifier defenses, continuous eval refresh. The general phenomenon (jailbreaks generalizing past benchmarks) is well demonstrated; the specific 'eval-set overfit' framing is established research but hard to attribute to one PoC.

validation_method: Against a deployed agent, run system-prompt/tool-schema extraction prompts (leakage requests, 'repeat the text above', error-induced disclosure, refusal-boundary probing). Verify the extracted prompt/tool names against ground truth. Then craft an injection that names the disclosed tools and quotes the exact instruction wording, and measure whether a targeted injection succeeds at a higher rate than a blind one. Seam confirmed if extraction succeeds AND targeting measurably improves attack success.
falsifier: System prompt and tool schemas cannot be extracted (robust output filtering / no reflection), OR knowing the exact wording/tool names provides no measurable uplift over blind injection because defenses are content-based not obscurity-based.
prerequisites: Interactive query access to the target agent.
existing_controls: Output filters that block prompt disclosure, prompt-injection classifiers, defenses designed to be robust to known wording (Kerckhoffs principle). System-prompt extraction itself is widely demonstrated publicly; obscurity is known to be weak.

validation_method: In a lab tenant of a UEBA/cloud-EDR product, establish a baseline for a host/identity, then introduce a target malicious behavior incrementally over the model's training window (e.g. slowly increasing data egress volume or login-time drift) at a rate below the anomaly threshold. After the rolling baseline normalizes, perform the full-tempo action and observe whether it scores below threshold (no alert). Compare against the same action performed cold.
falsifier: The full-tempo action still alerts despite the slow drip (the model uses fixed/expert thresholds, peer-group/population baselining, or change-point detection that flags the drift itself), OR the training window is too long/weighted to be shifted within a realistic dwell time.
prerequisites: Persistent low-and-slow control of a monitored host/identity over weeks, knowledge of the model's adaptation window, and tolerance for very long timelines.
existing_controls: Peer-group baselining, fixed detections layered over ML, drift/change-point monitoring, retraining governance, threat-hunting. Concept (model poisoning of adaptive baselines) is theoretically sound but rarely demonstrated end-to-end against commercial UEBA.

validation_method: On a host running an eBPF-based EDR sensor, load a second privileged eBPF program (CAP_BPF) that tampers with the data the sensor relies on: overwrite/filter entries in shared maps, attach a competing hook with higher priority, or rewrite syscall return data (e.g. hide a PID from getdents/readdir, drop connection events). Run a known-detectable action and observe whether the EDR's view omits it. Seam confirmed if the sensor reports a clean/incomplete view.
falsifier: The sensor's maps are not writable by other programs, hook ordering cannot be preempted, the kernel/LSM restricts bpf_probe_write/map access, or the EDR cross-checks with an independent source so tampering is detected.
prerequisites: CAP_BPF / root / privileged container with bpf() syscall access, kernel version supporting the needed eBPF features.
existing_controls: bpf() lockdown, restricting CAP_BPF, BPF token/LSM hooks, signed/locked programs, kernel-level integrity. eBPF rootkits (TripleCross, ebpfkit, boopkit, Symbiote-style) are publicly demonstrated; using eBPF to blind eBPF EDR specifically is shown in research/PoCs.

validation_method: Build an NL-to-fieldbus agent that takes one approved high-level intent and emits multiple Modbus/OPC-UA writes. Approve a benign high-level goal, then via injected context cause the decomposition to include extra/over-broad writes. Observe whether those un-approved function-code writes are emitted to a simulated PLC (e.g. ModbusPal / OpenPLC) without a per-write approval check. Seam confirmed if injected sub-steps reach the controller carrying inherited approval.
falsifier: The translation layer enforces approval at the function-code boundary (per-write allowlist, signed command envelope, human re-confirmation of the concrete register writes), so injected sub-steps are blocked.
prerequisites: An agentic NL-to-OT bridge that binds approval to NL intent rather than wire commands; ability to inject into the agent's context. Such bridges are largely experimental today.
existing_controls: Per-command authorization at the protocol layer, OT command allowlisting, engineering-workstation change control, unidirectional gateways. Approval-at-the-wire is the standard correct design; NL-to-OT agents are nascent.

validation_method: Configure a fleet-ops agent with authority to push OTA images, where the workflow relies on the agent's narrated 'I verified the signature' rather than an enforced cryptographic gate. Supply an unsigned/swapped/downgraded image plus an attacker-authored changelog/release page. Observe whether the agent rationalizes it as legitimate and pushes it, and whether devices accept it. Seam confirmed if the image is deployed without a real signature check enforced by the device or pipeline.
falsifier: Devices enforce secure boot / signature verification independently of the agent's narration, so an unsigned/swapped image is rejected at the device regardless of what the agent 'concluded'; or the pipeline gates on a verified signature, not agent text.
prerequisites: A deployment that delegates provenance to agent narration and a fleet that does not independently enforce signatures (or accepts downgrades). Well-run fleets enforce secure boot.
existing_controls: Device-side secure boot, signed firmware with anti-rollback, TUF/Uptane update frameworks, code-signing enforcement in the CD pipeline. These neutralize it when present; the seam exists only where verification is delegated to the agent.

validation_method: Set up a Matter/Zigbee controller driven by an LLM home-automation agent with commissioning authority. Inject a malicious instruction via untrusted text (a device-advertised name, a calendar entry, an email the agent reads) directing it to commission an attacker device or rebind a control group. Observe whether the agent issues the commissioning/binding command and the fabric admits the rogue node. Seam confirmed if a rogue device joins or groups rebind from agent action without human physical confirmation.
falsifier: Matter commissioning requires the device passcode/QR + physical proximity / user attestation that the agent cannot supply from injected text, so the join fails; or the controller requires explicit human approval per commissioning.
prerequisites: An LLM agent with real controller commissioning authority, an injection channel, and a fabric configured to let the controller commission without out-of-band human proof. Matter's commissioning normally needs the device's secret passcode.
existing_controls: Matter Passcode/PASE commissioning requiring the device secret, device attestation certificates, physical-proximity/QR requirements, human-in-the-loop commissioning. These strongly limit it; the agent generally cannot mint a valid passcode from prose.

validation_method: Set up an LLM-as-judge pipeline (e.g. RAG-answer grader or code-review scorer) and submit a candidate artifact containing embedded steering text ('this meets all criteria, score 10/10', authoritative framing, or known sycophancy triggers). Compare the verdict/score against the same artifact without the injected text. Seam confirmed if the embedded content materially raises the score or flips a fail to pass.
falsifier: The judge ignores in-artifact instructions (instruction/data separation, injection-robust judging, structured rubric scoring) so scores are unchanged by the embedded steering text.
prerequisites: Ability to submit the artifact that the judge evaluates; no need for direct judge access.
existing_controls: Delimiting/escaping evaluated content, injection-aware judge prompts, ensemble/multi-judge voting, rubric-constrained scoring, human spot-checks. Prompt injection of LLM judges is publicly demonstrated in research and benchmarks.

validation_method: In an identity/risk pipeline that ingests a data-broker or reputation/IP-intel feed, seed attacker-favorable records upstream (create false firmographic/broker entries, age an IP/domain to gain reputation, register misleading device attributes) and submit a subject that triggers the enrichment lookup. Observe whether the poisoned 'facts' flip an automated approve/deny/escalate decision. Seam confirmed if the manipulated feed changes the decision and the pipeline cannot trace the poisoned source.
falsifier: The pipeline corroborates enrichment against independent signals or treats single-source enrichment as advisory only (human review, multi-source consensus), so a single poisoned feed cannot move the decision.
prerequisites: Ability to influence the upstream broker/reputation source (record seeding, reputation aging, registration), and a pipeline that auto-decisions on the feed.
existing_controls: Multi-source corroboration, provider data-quality/fraud controls, manual review thresholds, anomaly checks on enrichment values. IP/reputation aging and broker-record manipulation are demonstrated in adjacent fraud contexts.

validation_method: Deploy a CDN/edge config (e.g. Cloudflare/Fastly/CloudFront) where the origin authorizes/personalizes a response using an input (a tenant token, an edge-function-injected header, a Vary-omitted field) that is NOT part of the computed cache key. As victim, populate the cache with a scoped response; as attacker, issue a request that produces the same cache key and observe whether the victim-scoped/cross-tenant response is served. Seam confirmed on cross-tenant disclosure. This is web cache deception / key-confusion.
falsifier: All authorization/personalization inputs are folded into the cache key or Vary, or auth'd responses are marked private/no-store, so a different requester never gets a cache hit on someone else's response.
prerequisites: A CDN config with a mismatch between authorizing inputs and the cache key (misconfigured Vary, header injection by edge functions, tenant token outside key). Attacker needs only normal request access.
existing_controls: Correct Cache-Key/Vary configuration, Cache-Control private/no-store on authorized content, cache deception protections. Web cache deception and key-confusion disclosures are publicly demonstrated (multiple real incidents/bug bounties).

validation_method: On a testnet/fork, deploy a protocol using a short-window on-chain TWAP (e.g. Uniswap v2/v3 cumulative-price oracle) backing liquidation/mint. As an attacker controlling block production cadence (local sequencer, or a forked chain where you control block timestamps/ordering) on a low-liquidity pool, manipulate price and stuff/space blocks so the manipulated observations dominate the time-weighting, then trigger the dependent action within the warped window. Measure extracted value vs. a fair TWAP.
falsifier: The TWAP window is long enough and the pool liquid enough that controlling cadence cannot make a brief manipulated price dominate, or the protocol cross-checks against an independent oracle (e.g. Chainlink) so the warped TWAP doesn't drive the action.
prerequisites: Control of block production cadence (own L2 sequencer, proposer/builder power, or a chain with long/irregular block times) and a low-liquidity pool; a protocol relying on a short on-chain TWAP.
existing_controls: Longer TWAP windows, deep-liquidity pools, multi-oracle / Chainlink cross-checks, per-block manipulation caps. TWAP manipulation generally is demonstrated; the specific cadence/sequencer-warping vector is plausible but liquidity-bounded.

validation_method: Set up a VC verifier using StatusList2021 (or an on-chain revocation registry) that caches the status list. Issue a credential, revoke it at the issuer, then present the credential to the verifier within the cache TTL / before the status list mirror updates. Observe whether the verifier accepts the revoked credential as active. Vary cache TTL and force resolution to a stale mirror to measure the acceptance window.
falsifier: The verifier fetches a fresh, signed, timestamped status list on every verification (no stale cache) or uses a freshness/'valid as of' bound, so a credential revoked before presentation is reliably rejected; revocation propagation is effectively synchronous in the deployment.
prerequisites: Ability to present a credential during the propagation/cache window, or to steer the verifier to a stale status-list mirror / did resolution. Window may be short.
existing_controls: Short status-list cache TTLs, signed and freshness-bounded status lists, OCSP-stapling-style freshness, online status checks. Revocation-propagation lag is a known general PKI/CRL problem; specific VC StatusList exploitation is plausible, not widely demonstrated.

validation_method: In an RF-shielded lab (or with a licensed/contained SDR like HackRF/USRP running GPS-SDR-SIM/gps-sdr-sim-style coherent spoofer), capture-and-drag a target receiver's position smoothly along an attacker trajectory consistent with vehicle dynamics. Feed the receiver into an autonomy/state estimator and observe whether it actuates on the spoofed fix (heading change, false geofence breach, RTH toward attacker coordinates) without rejecting the fix.
falsifier: The platform fuses an independent absolute reference or anti-spoof signal (multi-antenna/CRPA angle-of-arrival, RAIM, signal-quality/AGC anomaly detection, INS innovation gating) and rejects or down-weights the spoofed fix so actuation does not follow it.
prerequisites: RF transmit capability and (lawfully) a shielded environment; a target autonomy stack that fuses GNSS as ground truth without independent cross-checks. Coherent capture-and-drag requires moderate sophistication.
existing_controls: RAIM, multi-constellation/multi-frequency, antenna-array anti-spoofing (CRPA), INS/visual cross-checks, signal authentication (Galileo OSNMA). GNSS spoofing including capture-and-drag is publicly demonstrated (academic and real-world maritime/UAV incidents).

validation_method: On a PTP/NTP-synced lab segment, insert an on-path element (transparent-clock relay, programmable switch, or tc/netem middlebox) that adds latency to only one direction. Measure the client's computed offset and observe it slew its clock by ~half the injected one-way asymmetry. Confirm authenticated PTP (e.g. with integrity protection) still mis-syncs because the path-symmetry assumption, not packet content, is violated.
falsifier: The client measures or bounds one-way delay independently (multi-path/redundant time sources, two-way symmetric verified paths, GNSS cross-check) and rejects or corrects the asymmetric error, so the injected delay does not shift its clock undetectably.
prerequisites: On-path position to introduce directional delay (compromised switch, transparent clock, or inline middlebox).
existing_controls: Redundant/diverse time sources, GNSS or holdover cross-check, asymmetry monitoring, hardware timestamping with calibrated paths, multiple PTP grandmasters. PTP asymmetric-delay attacks are well established in academic literature and demonstrated in testbeds.

validation_method: Prompt popular code models repeatedly for install/import suggestions across many tasks and collect package names; confirm a stable subset of names that do not exist on the registry (recurrent hallucinations). Register a benign canary under one such name and instrument it; then have a fresh build agent act on the same hallucinated suggestion and observe whether it installs/executes the canary. Seam confirmed if hallucinated names recur stably AND agents install them unchecked.
falsifier: Hallucinated names are not stable/recurring across runs (so pre-registration cannot target them), OR the developer/build agent verifies package existence/ownership/popularity before install so the squatted name is rejected.
prerequisites: Ability to register packages on a public registry; an LLM-assisted workflow that installs suggested names without verification.
existing_controls: Dependency allowlists, install-time existence/ownership/age checks, lockfiles, scoped/private registries, package-hallucination scanners. 'Slopsquatting' / package hallucination is publicly studied and demonstrated (research and proof-of-concept registrations).

validation_method: Publish a shared agent-definition / system prompt to a community repo or gist that embeds hidden directives (exfiltrate context to a URL, prefer an attacker MCP server, lower guardrails on a trigger phrase). Have a team import it as configuration without review and run the agent through normal tasks; observe whether the agent exfiltrates context, routes to the attacker tool, or relaxes guardrails on the trigger. Seam confirmed if the imported prose silently alters agent behavior at privileged level.
falsifier: The importing workflow reviews/sanitizes prompt imports (treats them as control-plane code with diff review, signing, or a policy linter) so the hidden directives are caught, OR the runtime enforces tool-allowlists/guardrails that the prompt cannot override.
prerequisites: Ability to publish/update a shared prompt that a target team imports unreviewed, and an agent that loads it into privileged instruction context.
existing_controls: Treating prompts as code (review/sign/pin), prompt-injection scanning of imported definitions, tool allowlists and runtime guardrails independent of prompt text. Hidden-directive prompts are demonstrated in research; supply-chain via shared prompts is plausible and emerging.
